search.emptyBody: Try Ops Console, Patient Flow Link, Wait Room Display, or Clinic Dashboard.

footer.headline: Flow OS turns arrival, booking, consult, and follow-up into one operating journey
footer.deck: Aurora Derm acts as the reference clinic and the legacy turnero now lives inside Flow OS as the arrival, reception, and waiting-room module.

columns.title: V1 scope
lines: Assistive AI with explicit human approval

landing.title: Flow OS | Operating journey for dermatology clinics
landing.description: Flow OS coordinates capture, triage, booking, consult, and closure for a single-site ambulatory dermatology clinic.
landing.heading: Flow OS coordinates the patient journey end to end

hero.title: Coordinate the patient journey so the team stops duplicating work and the patient stops getting lost between capture, booking, consult, and follow-up.
hero.deck: Flow OS is the B2B product. Aurora Derm is the reference clinic we use to show the full journey over one tenant and one patient case.
pills: Aurora Derm as the reference clinic

metrics.label: captured -> closed
metrics.detail: The paid pilot is intentionally frozen into one clear operating journey.

metrics.label: Aurora Derm as reference
metrics.detail: Ops Console, patient link, waiting room, and dashboard read the same case and the same tenant.

metrics.label: primary sales and operating surface
metrics.detail: The commercial proof starts where the team works, not where the visuals look best.

panels.title: Callback, form, or reminder opens the same patient case
panels.copy: Lead capture does not create a second operating universe or a second backlog.

panels.title: Triage and scheduling stay attached to the live case
panels.copy: Front desk stops rebuilding context when it is time to book the visit.

panels.title: Check-in, queue, and consult room become timeline signals
panels.copy: The visit does not split into two systems or two sources of truth.

panels.title: Closure leaves follow-up prepared and leadership with a readable outcome
panels.copy: Leadership gets continuity without asking for a separate report.

focus.title: One product story for operations, leadership, and patient experience.
focus.deck: There is no need to sell isolated modules. Flow OS reads like one operating layer that keeps the full journey aligned.

cards.title: Ops Console concentrates the work that used to be split apart
cards.copy: Cases, callbacks, appointments, approvals, queue state, and prepared actions all sit on the same patient case.
items: One console for front desk and follow-up work
Valid transitions inside the five-stage journey
Traceability by tenant and by case

cards.title: Patient Flow Link explains what happens next without opening another system
cards.copy: The patient confirms arrival, sees the next step, and reads the same state front desk is already using.
items: Capture and check-in on the same case
A clear next step before the consult
Fewer repeated questions in the room

cards.title: Clinic Dashboard turns operations into a decision
cards.copy: The dashboard summarizes wait time, no-show, case attention, and backlog without needing a second data story.
items: KPI readout by tenant and workday
Recent cases and attention cases
A cleaner base for later module or site expansion

modules.title: Flow OS can already be walked as a complete product.
modules.deck: Ops Console is the lead surface. Patient Flow Link, Wait Room Display, and Clinic Dashboard all sit on the same tenant and the same patient case.

cards.title: Ops Console
cards.copy: The main console shows patient cases, callbacks, booking, approvals, outbox work, and exceptions without splitting operations across multiple tools.
bullets: Capture, triage, booking, consult, and closure in one read
AI-prepared actions with explicit human approval

cards.title: Patient Flow Link
cards.copy: The patient link exposes the next step, live queue context, and recent timeline while reading the same patient case.
bullets: Shared tenant, case, and timeline with Ops Console
Closes the gap between booking and visit day

cards.title: Wait Room Display
cards.copy: The waiting-room surface publishes the queue on the same tenant so front desk, room staff, and the patient all read the same progress.
bullets: Check-in and queue events land on the case timeline
Visible privacy and queue depth in one board
Continuity when the site needs a dedicated room display

cards.title: Clinic Dashboard
cards.copy: Leadership sees wait time, no-show, recent cases, and attention cases using exactly the same operating base as the rest of the product.
bullets: Same tenant, same counts, same stage readout
A clean base for later upsell into sites or extra modules

journeys.title: The paid pilot is sold as one fixed and understandable loop.
journeys.deck: Flow OS does not try to sell twelve states up front. The current commercial cut freezes a five-stage journey on one patient case.

lanes.title: A callback, form, or reminder opens the case
steps: Operations sees the new case without rebuilding it.
Aurora Derm demonstrates the same origin on a realistic tenant.

lanes.title: Triage and the next action are prepared on the case
steps: The team qualifies the case without moving it into another tool.
AI can prepare an action, but it does not run clinical work alone.
Human approval stays explicit where it matters.

lanes.title: A booked visit leaves the patient's next step ready
steps: Booking and patient link read the same patient case.
The patient does not receive a disconnected experience.
Front desk sees the same scheduled context it will later operate.

lanes.title: Check-in and queue become timeline signals, not a parallel database
steps: Arrival lands as a case event.
The room display publishes the same tenant and the same queue.
Ops Console and Wait Room Display follow the same progress.

lanes.title: Closure leaves follow-up ready and leadership with a usable readout
steps: The case closes without losing what happened across the journey.
Leadership can read load, wait time, and throughput without another report.
Multi-site or extra-module expansion stays as the next commercial step.

surfaces.deck: The sale starts in Ops Console and is reinforced by patient, waiting-room, and leadership surfaces on the same base.

cards.title: The patient understands what happens next on the same case

nativeApps.title: If the site needs local hardware, Flow OS keeps it inside the same contract
nativeApps.deck: Installable modules follow the same product base and keep an immediate web fallback.

integrations.title: V1 already has clear domains and a defined source of truth.
integrations.deck: There is no need to open a parallel layer to sell the pilot. Public domains are already defined and the case timeline absorbs callbacks, check-in, and queue.

pricing.title: One offer to close the first paid pilot
pricing.deck: At this stage we do not sell three plans. We sell one fixed-scope offer for a single-site ambulatory dermatology clinic.

plans.name: Flow OS Dermatology Pilot
plans.note: Fixed scope with Aurora Derm as the reference tenant
plans.fit: Ambulatory dermatology clinics that need to align capture, booking, check-in, consult, and closure without replacing their full clinical system.
features: V1 journey frozen into five stages
Ops Console, Patient Flow Link, Wait Room Display, and Clinic Dashboard
Reference tenant ready for repeatable commercial walkthroughs
Assistive AI with explicit human approval
Prepared for later upsell into modules or extra sites

security.title: The paid pilot is not sold as autonomous clinical automation.
security.deck: Commercial trust comes from being explicit: Flow OS coordinates operations, traceability, and approvals without claiming to replace a full EMR or run autonomous clinical decisions.

cards.title: One active patient case per patient
items: tenantId required in the runtime
Rule enforced by patient and tenant
Full journey traceability

cards.title: Assistive AI with human approval
items: Recommends and prepares actions
Not sold as clinical autonomy
Canonical review decisions to approve or reject

cards.title: Consistent data across surfaces
items: Ops Console, patient link, room display, and dashboard read the same tenant
Callbacks, queue, and check-in land on the same timeline
Audit and reports come from the same base

faq.title: Questions that come up before the first deployment is bought

items.question: Does Flow OS replace the full clinical system?
items.answer: No. In this cut we sell operational coordination of the patient journey, not EMR, billing, pharmacy, or lab.

items.question: Why does Aurora Derm appear in the sale?
items.answer: Because Aurora Derm acts as the reference clinic and demo tenant. The product being sold is Flow OS, not the clinic brand.

items.question: What happens to the current turnero?
items.answer: It stays in place as the arrival, reception, and waiting-room module inside Flow OS. It stops being the main buying story.

items.question: Does the twelve-state journey already belong in the paid pilot?
items.answer: No. The current commercial cut is frozen into five stages to reduce complexity and prove a sellable base before expanding the roadmap.

finalCta.title: The purchase starts with Flow OS and is demonstrated across one complete journey, not across a collection of screens.
finalCta.deck: Aurora Derm only plays the role of reference clinic. Ops Console leads the sale and the other surfaces prove coherence on the same tenant.
